sections.heading: Membership of securities exchange
sections.title: Membership requirements for securities exchanges
sections.summary: This section specifies that trading participants must be licensed by the Authority with rights to trade. It outlines admission requirements, limits on ownership by trading participants, and restrictions on holding beneficial interests in multiple trading participants to prevent conflicts of interest.

requirements.text: (1) Trading participants of a securities exchange shall be licensees of the Authority with rights to trade at an approved securities exchange.
(1A) The Authority may prescribe limits on the ownership of a securities exchange by its trading participants.

(2) A securities exchange may, in accordance with the procedures prescribed in its rules, admit as a trading participant, any person who has been licensed by the Authority to exercise rights to trade—
(a) if that person satisfies any admission requirements of the securities exchange; and
(b) on payment of admission fee approved by the Authority under section 29(2) of the Act, and accord that person the applicable rights to the relevant category of admission:

Provided that, a securities exchange may assess an application for admission by a person seeking a licence from the Authority and issue a confirmation that that person shall be admitted upon securing a license from the Authority.

(3) A trading participant of a securities exchange or a director or a shareholder of a trading participant shall not be a director or hold beneficial interest either directly or indirectly in more than one trading participant of a securities exchange unless the trading participants has been exempted by the Authority on the basis of evidence of adequate internal controls to address conflict of interest.

(4) In case of a listed trading participant of a securities exchange, an interest of fifteen per cent or more of the voting shares held directly or indirectly shall be deemed to be a person's beneficial interest for purpose of these Regulations.

sections.heading: Chairman, directors and chief executive
sections.title: Chief executive qualifications and board structure requirements
sections.summary: Securities exchanges must appoint a chief executive with at least ten years of senior management experience in law, finance, accounting, economics, banking, or insurance, and expertise in money, capital markets, or finance. The chief executive must have a fixed term of office (three years renewable once for chairman, four years renewable once for chief executive). Exchanges must establish a board with independent non-executive directors representing investors and public interest, elected by shareholders from trading participants, and ensure appointments are confirmed by the Authority.

sections.heading: Stockbrokers' financial requirements
sections.title: Stockbrokers' financial capital requirements
sections.summary: Stockbrokers must maintain shareholders' funds (paid-up share capital and reserves) of at least fifty million shillings at all times during their license period. Their paid-up share capital must remain unimpaired and cannot be advanced to directors or associates. They must also maintain a liquid capital of thirty million shillings or eight percent of total liabilities, whichever is higher.

sections.heading: Dealers' financial requirements and investment limits
sections.title: Dealers' financial requirements and investment limits
sections.summary: Dealers must maintain shareholders' funds of at least twenty million shillings during their license period. They must set aside investment capital of at least twenty million shillings in cash or listed securities, with exceptions for subsidiaries promoted by stockbrokers. Dealers must also maintain a liquid capital of thirty million shillings or eight percent of total liabilities, whichever is higher, and maintain investment portfolios with a minimum monthly average of fifty percent in listed equities (adjustable to sixty percent within twelve months).
sections.dtia_summary: Defines financial and investment requirements for dealers, which affect data storage, processing, and risk management practices.

requirements.text: The level of shareholders funds (paid-up share capital and reserves) shall not be below twenty million shillings, at any time during the licence period

Where a dealer is promoted by a stockbroker through a subsidiary, the minimum investment capital committed to dealing operations by the subsidiary shall not be less than five million shillings in cash or listed securities portfolio at market value or such higher amount as may be prescribed by the Authority
A dealer shall maintain a liquid capital of thirty million shillings or eight per cent of its total liabilities, whichever is higher

A dealer shall maintain an investment portfolio out of its investment capital equivalent to a minimum monthly average of fifty per cent in listed equities and the remainder in listed fixed income securities provided that within twelve months from the date of these Regulations, the investment of the minimum monthly average in listed equities shall be adjusted to sixty per cent

At least an average of twenty-five per cent of the portfolio of securities held by a dealer shall be turned over every quarter and seventy-five per cent of the portfolio be turned over every twelve months
Every security held by a dealer shall be turned over at least once every eighteen months

A dealer shall maintain an investment portfolio out of its investment capital equivalent to a minimum monthly average of fifty per cent in listed equities and the remainder in listed fixed income securities provided that within twelve months from the date of these Regulations, the investment of the minimum monthly average in listed equities shall be adjusted to sixty per cent. (a) At least an average of twenty-five per cent of the portfolio of securities held by a dealer shall be turned over every quarter and seventy-five per cent of the portfolio be turned over every twelve months. sections.heading: Client accounts
sections.title: Client account management requirements
sections.summary: Stockbrokers must deposit clients' funds in dedicated bank accounts marked 'clients' accounts', which cannot be overdrawn. They must maintain separate records for each client account showing bank details, deposit/withdrawal history, and beneficial interest. Regular reconciliations are required to ensure account balances match client funds, and client orders for advance payments must be executed within one month of fund receipt.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires strict client account management practices to ensure data security and compliance with financial transactions.

requirements.text: A stockbroker shall deposit clients' funds in one or more bank account(s), which account(s) shall contain only clients' funds and be clearly marked 'clients' accounts'. Such client accounts shall not be overdrawn for any reason

A stockbroker shall maintain a separate record for each account showing the name and address of the bank where the account is maintained, the dates, amounts of deposits and withdrawals and also the exact amount of each client's beneficial interest in the account
A stockbroker shall reconcile such accounts on a regular basis to ensure the amount indicated corresponds with the balances in the client account at any given time

A stockbroker shall ensure that clients' orders for payments made in advance shall be executed according to clients' instructions and in any event not later that one month from the date of receipt of the clients' funds. Orders not executed within one month for whatever reason shall be renewed by fresh instructions from the client

sections.source_quote: A stockbroker shall— (a) deposit clients' funds in one or more bank account(s), which account(s) shall contain only clients' funds and be clearly marked "clients' accounts". Such client accounts shall not be overdrawn for any reason; (b) maintain a separate record for each account showing the name and address of the bank where the account is maintained, the dates, amounts of deposits and withdrawals and also the exact amount of each client's beneficial interest in the account; (c) reconcile such accounts on a regular basis to ensure the amount indicated corresponds with the balances in the client account at any given time; and (d) ensure that clients' orders for payments made in advance shall be executed according to clients' instructions and in any event not later that one month from the date of receipt of the clients' funds. Orders not executed within one month for whatever reason shall be renewed by fresh instructions from the client.

sections.source_quote: 31. Records to be maintained (1) Every investment adviser and fund manager shall maintain and preserve for a period of seven years, the following records— (a) journals, including cash receipts and disbursement records and any other records or original entry, forming the basis of entries in any ledger; (b) general and auxiliary ledgers, or other comparable records reflecting assets, liabilities, reserves, capital, income and expense accounts; (c) a record or memorandum of each order given by the investment adviser or fund manager for the purchase or sale of securities, or any instruction received by the investment adviser or fund manager from the client concerning the purchase, sale, receipt or delivery of a particular security and of any modification or cancellation or any such order or instruction, and the record shall— (i) show the terms and conditions of the order, instruction, modification or cancellation; (ii) identify the person connected with the investment adviser or fund manager who recommended the transaction to the client and the person who placed such order; (iii) show the account for which the order was entered, the date of entry, and the stockbroker by or through whom the order was executed, where appropriate; and (iv) show orders entered pursuant to the exercise of discretionary power on account of management of investment portfolios in which case a record of details of such contracts with clients, constituents of the portfolio, transaction fees agreed with the client and value of the portfolio shall be included; (d) all cheque books, bank statements, cancelled cheques and cash reconciliation of the investment adviser or fund manager; (e) all bills, statements or copies thereof, paid or unpaid relating to the business of the investment adviser or the fund manager; (f) originals of all written communication received from clients and copies of all written communication sent by the investment adviser or fund manager relating to— (i) any recommendations made or proposed to be given; (ii) any receipts, disbursement or delivery of funds or securities; and (iii) the placing or execution of any order to purchase or sell any security; provided, that if the investment adviser or fund manager sends any notice, circular or other advertisement offering any report, analysis, publication or other investment advisory services to more than ten persons, the investment adviser or fund manager shall not be required to keep a record of the names and addresses of the persons to whom it was sent except that if such notice, circular or advertisement is distributed to persons named on any list, the investment adviser or fund manager shall retain a copy of such notice, circular or advertisement, a record or memorandum describing the list and the source; (g) all written agreements or copies thereof entered into with clients; (h) a copy of each notice, circular, advertisement, etc. recommending purchase or sale of securities; (i) a record of every transaction in a security in which the investment adviser or fund manager or any employee acquires beneficial ownership; (j) a copy of each written statement, amendment or revision given to clients; (k) the custodian appointed; (l) the securities of each client segregated and marked to identify the particular client having the beneficial interest therein.

(4) An investment adviser or fund manager may not contract or engage any advisory or management services on behalf of an investment portfolio without prior written approval of its clients. requirements.text: The level of paid-up share capital shall not fall below two hundred and fifty million shillings at any time during the licence period
Shareholders' funds (paid up share capital and Reserves) shall at no time fall below two hundred and fifty million shillings
Any investment bank whose paid-up share capital is below the required amount at the time of commencement of this paragraph shall comply by the 31st December 2010
The minimum paid-up share capital shall always be unimpaired and shall not be advanced to the directors or associates of the investment bank
An investment bank shall maintain a liquid capital of thirty million or eight per cent of its total liabilities, whichever is higher

sections.source_quote: (1) The level of paid-up share capital shall not fall below two hundred and fifty million shillings at any time during the licence period and in addition, shareholders' funds (paid up share capital and Reserves) shall at no time fall below two hundred and fifty million shillings: Provided that any investment bank whose paid-up share capital is below the required amount at the time of commencement of this paragraph shall comply by the 31st December 2010.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure financial regulation, no data protection or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: Application for licence
sections.title: Application requirements for securities dealers
sections.summary: To apply for a license as an authorized securities dealer, applicants must be licensed banks, investment banks, fund managers, or insurance companies meeting specific criteria, including demonstrating effective capacity and expertise in securities dealing. Applicants must also provide evidence of financial resources and submit a business plan detailing management structure, directors, and operational infrastructure.
sections.dtia_summary: Outlines application criteria for securities dealers, impacting data transfer requirements for financial institutions operating across borders.

requirements.text: An application for a licence to operate as an authorized securities dealer shall be submitted to the Authority in Form 1
An applicant shall be a bank licensed under the Banking Act (Cap. 488)
An applicant shall be an investment bank or a fund manager
An applicant shall be an insurance company licensed under the Insurance Act (Cap. 487)
An applicant shall demonstrate effective capacity and expertise in dealing in securities
An authorized securities dealer who intends to be admitted as a trading participant at a securities exchange shall submit a letter from the securities exchange confirming admission

sections.source_quote: An application for a licence to operate as an authorized securities dealer shall be submitted to the Authority in Form 1 set out in the First Schedule.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: financial entity licensing criteria

sections.heading: Specific requirements for approval
sections.title: Application documentation for securities dealers
sections.summary: Applications for securities dealer licenses require submission of incorporation certificates, memorandum and articles of association, evidence of minimum paid-up share capital, financial capability evidence, un-audited accounts, and a business plan detailing management structure, directors, qualifications, and proposed operational infrastructure.
sections.dtia_summary: Specifies documentation requirements for securities dealers, influencing data storage and processing capabilities for cross-border financial operations.

requirements.text: The application shall be submitted together with the certificate of incorporation
The application shall be submitted together with the memorandum and articles of association
The application shall be submitted together with evidence of the minimum paid up share capital prescribed by the Authority
The application shall be submitted together with evidence of the minimum financial resources and financial capability prescribed by the Authority

The application shall be submitted together with a statement of the un-audited accounts for the period of the accounting year ending not earlier than six months prior to the date of application and the applicant's audited accounts for the preceding two years (where applicable)
The application shall be submitted together with a business plan containing the particulars on management and shareholding structure of the applicant
The application shall be submitted together with a business plan containing the particulars on directors, including their qualifications, addresses and details of other directorships

The application shall be submitted together with a business plan containing the particulars on qualifications, experience and expertise of the chief dealer relevant to managing fixed income securities
The application shall be submitted together with a business plan containing the particulars on the proposed operating system including dealing infrastructure

sections.source_quote: The application under regulation 45 shall be submitted together with— (a) the certificate of incorporation; (b) the memorandum and articles of association; (c) evidence of the minimum paid up share capital prescribed by the Authority;
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data submission for licensing

sections.heading: Functions and membership on a securities exchange
sections.title: Securities dealer functions and exchange membership eligibility
sections.summary: Authorized securities dealers must trade only in fixed income securities, trade on behalf of others and self, and implement operational procedures to minimize settlement and counterparty risk while managing conflicts of interest. Eligible dealers must meet exchange-specific requirements to apply for trading participant status. Dealers must comply with client account and conduct of business regulations applicable to stockbrokers and dealers.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires dealers to restrict trading to fixed income securities and implement risk management systems, impacting cross-border data transfers involving securities transactions.

requirements.text: Restricted to dealing in fixed income securities whether listed on an approved exchange or not
Entitled to trade on behalf of others as well as on their own account in such segment
Required to implement necessary operational, trading and settlement procedures and systems to minimize settlement and counterparty risk and manage conflicts of interest
Meet eligibility requirements of the admitting securities exchange to apply for trading participant status
Comply with client accounts, conduct of business, prohibited dealings and associations and investment requirements regulations

sections.source_quote: 48. Functions and membership on a securities exchange (1) An authorized securities dealer shall be— (a) restricted to dealing in fixed income securities whether listed on an approved exchange or not; (b) entitled to trade on behalf of others as well as on their own account in such segment; and (c) required to implement necessary operational, trading and settlement procedures and systems necessary to minimize settlement and counterparty risk and manage conflicts of interest.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulatory requirements for financial data handling

sections.heading: Records of transactions
sections.title: Transaction records maintenance for securities dealers
sections.summary: Authorized securities dealers must maintain daily records of transactions including security type, trade value, counterparty, and account nature. These records supplement existing client account and conduct of business compliance requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates detailed transaction records that could impact data storage and transfer requirements for cross-border securities transactions.

requirements.text: Maintain a record of daily dealing transactions including particulars on type of security
Include value of trade in records
Include counterparty details in records
Include nature of account details in records

sections.source_quote: 49. Records of transactions In addition to the requirements specified under regulation 48(3) every authorized securities dealer shall maintain a record of its daily dealing transactions which shall include particulars on— (a) type of security; (b) value of trade; (c) counter party; and (d) nature of account.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data record-keeping for compliance

sections.heading: Report of dealing transactions
sections.title: Securities transaction reporting obligations
sections.summary: Authorized securities dealers must submit monthly, quarterly, half-yearly, and annual financial reports to the Capital Markets Authority within specified timeframes. Reports must include security type, trade value, and average yield metrics for the relevant period.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires regular financial reporting that impacts data retention and transfer for cross-border securities transactions.

requirements.text: Submit monthly reports and accounts within fifteen days of the end of each calendar month
Submit quarterly reports and accounts within fifteen days of the end of each calendar quarter
Submit half yearly reports and accounts within thirty days of the end of each year
Submit audited annual accounts within three months following the end of the authorized securities dealer financial year
Submit a financial statement complying with disclosures prescribed under the Fourth Schedule
Include particulars on type of securities in reports
Include total value of securities traded in terms of sales and purchases during the relevant period
Include average yield of total value of securities traded during the relevant period

sections.source_quote: 50. Report of dealing transactions (1) Every authorised securities dealer shall, in respect of all its transactions in securities, whether or not such securities are traded on an approved exchange, submit to the Authority— (a) monthly reports and accounts within fifteen days of the end of each calendar month; (b) quarterly reports and accounts within fifteen days of the end of each calendar quarter; (c) half yearly reports and accounts within thirty days of the end of each year; (d) audited annual accounts within three months following the end of the authorized securities dealer financial year; and (e) a financial statement complying with the disclosures prescribed under the Fourth Schedule of these Regulations.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial reporting obligations with data sharing

sections.heading: Renewal of licence
sections.title: Renewal of licence application requirements
sections.summary: Licensed securities dealers must submit renewal applications to the Authority by November 30 each year using Form 1. Applications require payment of specified fees and management accounts for the period up to November 30, submitted no later than December 15. Annual accounts must be submitted by March 31, and financial years end on December 31. Licensed persons may adjust their financial year within twelve months of the regulation's commencement.

requirements.text: Submit renewal application to Authority by November 30 each year using Form 1
Submit fees specified in Second Schedule with application
Submit management accounts for period up to November 30 by December 15
Submit audited annual accounts by March 31
Financial year ends on December 31
May adjust financial year within twelve months of commencement

sections.source_quote: 51. Renewal of licence (1) An application for the renewal of a licence shall be submitted to the Authority in Form 1 set out in the First Schedule by the 30th of November of each year.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential financial reporting requirements

sections.heading: Determination of suitability
sections.title: Fit and proper determination criteria
sections.summary: The Authority determines suitability for positions by assessing probity, competence, sound judgment, diligence in fulfilling responsibilities, potential threats to customer interests, contraventions of laws protecting against financial loss, involvement in irregularities, convictions for economic crimes, and history with liquidated brokerage firms.

requirements.text: Assess probity, competence and soundness of judgment in fulfilling responsibilities
Assess diligence in fulfilling or likely to fulfil responsibilities
Assess whether customer interests are threatened by holding position due to past convictions, irregularities, misappropriation or market manipulation
Assess contraventions of laws protecting against financial loss due to dishonesty or incompetence
Assess history with liquidated or liquidating brokerage firms
Assess involvement in fraudulent, prejudicial or improper business practices
Assess whether competence and soundness of judgment are compromised
Assess convictions for economic crimes under Anti-Corruption and Economic Crimes Act

sections.source_quote: 52. Determination of suitability In determining whether a person is fit and proper to hold any particular position, regard shall be had to—
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data fit and proper assessment criteria

sections.heading: Key personnel of full and associate members
sections.title: Key personnel registration requirements
sections.summary: Trading participants must register key personnel annually with the Authority, including any changes. Key personnel includes employees and directors who have direct client dealings and carry out trading activities on behalf of clients.

requirements.text: Register key personnel annually with Authority including any changes
Key personnel includes employees and directors with direct client dealings and trading activities

sections.source_quote: 53. Key personnel of full and associate members (1) All trading participants of a securities exchange shall register with the Authority all key personnel annually including any changes thereto.
sections.relevance_reason: Key personnel registration affects data handling

sections.heading: Alteration of memorandum or articles of association
sections.title: Submission of memorandum or articles alterations within 30 days
sections.summary: Licensed persons must submit any alterations to their memorandum or articles of association to the Authority within thirty days of approving such changes. This requirement applies to all licensed entities operating under the Capital Markets (Licensing Requirements) (General) Regulations. The Authority is the regulatory body responsible for this submission process.

requirements.text: Every licensed person shall submit to the Authority any alterations to its memorandum or articles of association within thirty days of passing the resolution approving such alteration.

sections.source_quote: Every licensed person shall submit to the Authority any alterations to its memorandum or articles of association within thirty days of passing the resolution approving such alteration.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential governance requirement for licensed entities

sections.heading: Qualification of Secretary
sections.title: Secretary qualification under Certified Public Secretaries Act
sections.summary: Licensed persons cannot engage a Secretary who is not qualified under the Institute of Certified Public Secretaries of Kenya Act (Cap. 534). This requirement applies to all licensed entities operating under the Capital Markets (Licensing Requirements) (General) Regulations. The Authority is the regulatory body responsible for this qualification process.

requirements.text: No licensed person shall engage as a Secretary a person who is not qualified under the Institute of Certified Public Secretaries of Kenya Act (Cap. 534).

sections.source_quote: No licensed person shall engage as a Secretary a person who is not qualified under the Institute of Certified Public Secretaries of Kenya Act (Cap. 534).
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: Secretary qualification not directly related to data processing

sections.heading: Nature of transaction
sections.title: Eligible private transactions for approval
sections.summary: Private transactions are eligible for Authority approval if they involve transfers to close relations as gifts, settlement of wills or estates, restructuring/mergers/acquisitions approved by the Authority, transfers of exceptional listed securities for strategic investors, or transfers that do not change beneficial ownership except as specified.

requirements.text: A private transaction is eligible for Authority approval if it involves a transfer to a close relation in the form of a gift.
A private transaction is eligible for Authority approval if it involves settlement of a will or estate of a deceased person.
A private transaction is eligible for Authority approval if it involves restructuring, mergers or acquisitions in a scheme approved by the Authority.

A private transaction is eligible for Authority approval if it involves a transfer of an exceptional nature of a listed security that the Authority considers proper and acceptable for a strategic investor serving public interest.

A private transaction is eligible for Authority approval if it does not result in any change in beneficial ownership otherwise than for purposes of regulation 57(c), (d) or section 31(1A)(ii) of the Act.

sections.source_quote: An application to the Authority for approval of a private transaction shall be considered if the transaction is for the— (a) transfer to a close relation in the form of a gift; (b) settlement of a will or estate of a deceased person; (c) restructuring, mergers or acquisitions in a scheme which has been approved by the Authority; (d) transfer of an exceptional nature of a listed security that the Authority considers to be proper and acceptable with respect to a strategic investor and serves the investor or public interest; or (e) transfer not resulting in any change in beneficial ownership otherwise than for purposes of regulation 57(c), (d) or section 31(1A)(ii) of the Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator approval for financial transactions

sections.heading: Application for approval of a private transfer
sections.title: Application for private transfer approval
sections.summary: For private transactions of listed securities, stockbrokers must submit applications to exchanges or depositories with supporting documents. Exchanges/depositories must notify the Authority within seven days whether they object to the transaction. The Authority approves applications for transactions under regulation 57(a) or (b) after exchanges/depositories confirm eligibility.

requirements.text: Where it is intended to effect a private transaction of a listed security under regulation 57(a), (b) and (e), a stockbroker representing the proposed transferee shall assess, endorse and submit a written application with required information and supporting documents.
The securities exchange or central depository shall notify the stockbroker within seven days of receiving the application whether it objects to the private transaction.
The securities exchange or central depository shall approve and notify the Authority that the application complies with regulation 57(a) or (b).
The securities exchange and central depository shall forward applications under regulation 57(e) to the Authority for approval.

sections.source_quote: Where it is intended to effect a private transaction of a listed security under regulation 57(a), (b) and (e), a stockbroker representing the proposed transferee shall assess, endorse and submit a written application with the required information and supporting documents— (a) in the case of certificated securities, to the securities exchange where the security is listed, and (b) in the case of immobilized securities, to the central depository at which the security is immobilized, stating reasons why the proposed transaction is eligible to be transferred in a private transaction.
sections.relevance_reason: Third-party processing for financial data

sections.heading: Private transactions under section 31(1A) of the Act
sections.title: Application for private transactions under section 31(1A) of the Act
sections.summary: For private transactions under section 31(1A)(ii) of the Act, applicants must submit detailed draft information memorandums or circulars to shareholders containing specific information about the applicant, core activities, directors, management, shareholders, agreements, financial adviser statements, merger details, and director declarations.

requirements.text: The applicant shall submit to the Authority a detailed draft information memorandum or circular to shareholders containing information on the name and address of the applicant, date of incorporation, core activities, directors, management, major shareholders, agreements, financial adviser statements, merger details, and director declarations.
The applicant shall make a public announcement of its intention to apply to the Authority for approval of the proposed transfer and reasons therein.

sections.source_quote: With respect to an application for approval of a private transaction, falling under regulation 57(c) or (d) or section 31(1A)(ii) of the Act, the applicant shall submit to the Authority for approval a detailed draft information memorandum or a circular to be distributed to the shareholders containing information on— (a) the name and address of the applicant; (b) the date of incorporation; (c) the particulars of core activities, directors, management and major shareholders; (d) the details of any agreements entered or proposed to be entered into and the cost; (e) a statement by the financial adviser managing the transaction that to the best of its knowledge and belief the application constitutes full and true disclosure of all material facts about the offer and issuer and where appropriate it has satisfied itself that the profit forecasts have been stated by the directors after due and careful inquiry; (f) the details of any proposed merger, takeover, acquisitions, share, swap, reorganization or restructure scheme and the relevant shareholders and/or board resolutions; (g) a declaration by the directors of the applicant in the following form: "This application has been approved by the directors of the company all of whom jointly and severally accept responsibility for the accuracy of the information given and confirm that after making all reasonable inquiries and to the best of their knowledge and belief, there are no facts the omission of which would make any statement herein misleading."; (h) any other matters as may be requested by the Authority.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data submission and public announcement requirements

sections.heading: Content of public communication and circular to shareholders
sections.title: Content requirements for public communications
sections.summary: Circulars to shareholders and public communications must be factual and serve to assist in security evaluation, promote the industry, or provide accurate information about listed companies. No material fact may be omitted if such omission would cause the communication to be misleading. Licensed persons must disclose price, recommendations, risk factors, and sources of facts in their communications.

requirements.text: All circulars to shareholders and the public including advertisements, offer documents and any other communication by listed companies, professionals and persons licensed under the Act shall be factual and statements made shall be for the purpose of assisting in the evaluation of a particular security, promoting the industry, or providing accurate information about listed companies.
No material fact or qualification may be omitted if such omission would cause a shareholders' circular, advertisement or offer document to be misleading.

In making a recommendation with respect to any security, a licensed person, issuer or analyst shall disclose the price at the time of the recommendation and whether they make a market in the securities.

In making a recommendation with respect to any security, a licensed person, issuer or analyst shall recommend a buy or sell action and disclose the basic facts and assumptions in support of such recommendation.

In making a recommendation with respect to any security, a licensed person, issuer or analyst shall highlight all risk factors that such licensed person or analyst has taken into consideration in the recommendation.
In making a recommendation with respect to any security, a licensed person, issuer or analyst shall state the source of the facts and the recommended time frame for the validity of assumptions.

sections.source_quote: All circulars to shareholders and the public including advertisements, offer documents and any other communication by listed companies, professionals and persons licensed under the Act shall be factual and statements made shall be for the purpose of— (a) assisting in the evaluation of a particular security, or type of securities; (b) promoting the industry, the service offered or the desirability of investing in securities in general; or (c) providing shareholders or the public with accurate and adequate information about the listed company or securities transaction and market activity.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data disclosure requirements for public communications

sections.heading: Contribution by licensees
sections.title: Stockbrokers and dealers must contribute to Compensation Fund
sections.summary: Trading participants in securities exchanges must contribute to the Compensation Fund in amounts prescribed by the Authority. Contributions are credited to a dedicated bank account managed by the Authority. This obligation applies to stockbrokers and dealers licensed to trade on securities exchanges.
sections.dtia_summary: Contribution requirements establish a financial safeguard mechanism for investor compensation, relevant to cross-border financial data transfers involving stockbrokers and dealers.

requirements.text: Every buying or selling stockbroker or dealer that is a trading participant of a securities exchange shall contribute to the Compensation Fund such amount as shall be prescribed from time to time by the Authority
All monies contributed to the Compensation Fund shall be credited to a bank account established by the Authority for that purpose

sections.source_quote: 64. Contribution by licensees (1) Every buying or selling stockbroker or dealer that is a trading participant of a securities exchange shall contribute to the Compensation Fund such amount as shall be prescribed from time to time by the Authority.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data contribution to compensation fund

sections.heading: Management and audit of the Compensation Fund
sections.title: Compensation Fund management and audit requirements
sections.summary: The Authority must manage the Compensation Fund as a separate entity, disclose it in annual financial statements, maintain proper accounts, and prepare statements of accounts showing fund movements. The Fund's accounts must include all contributions, expenses, and management fees. The Authority must also audit the Fund annually.
sections.dtia_summary: Clear financial safeguards for investor compensation, relevant to data transfer impact assessments involving financial institutions and cross-border fund management.

requirements.text: The Compensation Fund shall be managed by the Authority as a separate fund and disclosed as such in the Authority's annual balance sheet as an asset and liability

The Authority shall keep proper accounts and records of the Compensation Fund and in every financial year, prepare a statement of accounts showing the movement and financial position of the Fund in the Authority's annual report

The accounts referred to in paragraph (2) shall include the income and all sources of contribution to and expenses or disbursements of the Compensation Fund including the fees charged by the Authority for the management of the Fund and any investments of the Fund
The accounts and records of the Compensation Fund shall be audited by the auditor appointed by the Authority for the Authority's annual accounts

sections.source_quote: 65. Management and audit of the Compensation Fund (1) The Compensation Fund shall be managed by the Authority as a separate fund and disclosed as such in the Authority's annual balance sheet as an asset and liability.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data audit requirements

sections.heading: Report to the Cabinet Secretary
sections.title: Annual report inclusion of Compensation Fund details
sections.summary: The Authority must include information about the Compensation Fund in its annual report to the Cabinet Secretary responsible for Finance. This requirement ensures transparency and accountability in fund management to stakeholders.
sections.dtia_summary: Relevant for financial data transfers as it mandates reporting of compensation fund details to government authorities, impacting data retention and disclosure requirements.

requirements.text: The Authority shall include information relating to the Compensation Fund in its annual report to the Cabinet Secretary for the time being responsible for Finance

sections.source_quote: 68. Report to the Cabinet Secretary The Authority shall include information relating to the Compensation Fund in its annual report to the Cabinet Secretary for the time being responsible for Finance.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential governance for financial data reporting

sections.heading: Compensation of investors
sections.title: Investor compensation from Compensation Fund
sections.summary: Investors who suffer pecuniary loss due to a licensed stockbroker or dealer's failure to meet obligations may claim compensation from the Compensation Fund. The claim process requires the investor to apply to the Authority for payment equal to the net loss after exhausting other remedies like bank guarantees or statutory manager payments.
sections.dtia_summary: Directly relevant to data transfer impact assessments as it establishes a compensation mechanism for financial data breaches, affecting cross-border investor data handling.

requirements.text: Whenever an investor has suffered pecuniary loss due to the failure of a stockbroker, dealer or on investment bank carrying out stockbroking business or dealing, operations, to meet its contractual obligations, which loss has not been compensated— the investor shall apply to the Authority for compensation from the Compensation Fund in cash or securities equal to the net loss

sections.source_quote: 69. Compensation of investors Whenever an investor has suffered pecuniary loss due to the failure of a stockbroker, dealer or on investment bank carrying out stockbroking business or dealing, operations, to meet its contractual obligations, which loss has not been compensated— the investor shall apply to the Authority for compensation from the Compensation Fund in cash or securities equal to the net loss.
sections.relevance_reason: Compensation fund claims for financial loss

sections.heading: Investor Compensation Committee
sections.title: Investor Compensation Committee operations
sections.summary: The Authority must establish an Investor Compensation Committee to handle investor claims. The Committee includes the Chairman and Chief Executive of the Nairobi Stock Exchange, and may appoint additional members. It evaluates claims, recommends compensation amounts, and considers the fund's available balance before making final decisions.
sections.dtia_summary: Critical for DTIAs as it outlines a structured process for compensating investors, impacting data transfer safeguards for financial institutions.

requirements.text: The Authority shall establish an Investor Compensation Committee to deal with claims from investors
The Compensation Committee shall include the Chairman and the Chief Executive of the Nairobi Stock Exchange and any other persons who may be appointed by the Authority to be members of the Committee

The Compensation Committee shall, after examination of the evidence produced in support of a claim, make any recommendation to the Authority with respect to whether to allow or disallow such claim and, if the recommendation is to allow the claim, an assessment of the amount payable including any pro rata allocation of any such limit prescribed for every defaulting stockbroker or dealer or the size of the fraud, as applicable
While determining the amount to be paid in compensation to an Investor, the Compensation Committee shall take into account the total amount available in the Compensation Fund
The Authority shall give notice of its decision to the investors in writing or by other means of appropriate notification

sections.source_quote: 71. Investor Compensation Committee (1) The Authority shall establish an Investor Compensation Committee to deal with claims from investors.
sections.relevance_reason: Committee operations for financial claims handling

sections.heading: Notification of pecuniary loss
sections.title: Notification of pecuniary loss to statutory manager
sections.summary: Investors who suffer pecuniary loss must notify the statutory manager of the liable licensed person within sixty days of the manager's appointment. The statutory manager must pay all valid claims within six months of appointment.
sections.dtia_summary: Relevant for DTIAs as it establishes timelines for reporting financial losses, impacting data transfer and breach notification procedures.

requirements.text: Every investor who has suffered a pecuniary loss shall notify the statutory manager of the licensed person liable for the loss within sixty days of the appointment of the statutory manager
The statutory manager shall pay all valid claims within six months of its appointment

sections.source_quote: 72. Notification of pecuniary loss (1) Every investor who has suffered a pecuniary loss shall notify the statutory manager of the licensed person liable for the loss within sixty days of the appointment of the statutory manager.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulatory duty for financial data claims

sections.heading: Payment of claims
sections.title: Compensation fund liability for payments made to investors
sections.summary: Licensed persons must repay the Compensation Fund for any payments made from it to investors. If a licensed person goes insolvent, their liquidator must return any funds the Fund paid to investors. This section establishes financial accountability for compensation fund disbursements and ensures investor protection through fund recovery mechanisms.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires licensed entities to maintain financial liability for compensation fund payments, impacting cross-border financial data transfers involving investor protection mechanisms.

requirements.text: Licensed person must repay Compensation Fund for payments made from it to investors
Liquidator must return funds paid by Fund to investors during liquidation

sections.source_quote: Where payment has been made out of the Compensation Fund on behalf of a licensed person, such licensed person shall be liable to the Compensation Fund for an amount equal to the payment made out of the Fund. In the event of liquidation of a licensed person, the liquidator shall pay the Compensation Fund any money paid by the Fund to investors on behalf of the insolvent person under these Regulations to the extent of such payment.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential: financial accountability for compensation fund

sections.heading: Disclosure of interest in shares
sections.title: Share interest disclosure obligations for listed companies
sections.summary: Shareholders must notify listed companies of notifiable share interests (3% or more) when acquiring or ceasing to hold shares. Listed companies must report monthly to exchanges on notified interests, director holdings, and cumulative director shares. Certain individuals (employees, directors, spouses, children, and related entities) are automatically considered interested parties without requiring active notification.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires disclosure of share interests for listed companies, impacting data sharing and cross-border financial transparency in securities markets.

requirements.text: Shareholders must notify listed companies of notifiable share interests (3% or more) when acquiring or ceasing to hold shares
Listed companies must report monthly to exchanges on notified interests, director holdings, and cumulative director shares
Employees, directors, spouses, children, and related entities are automatically considered interested parties without requiring active notification

sections.source_quote: Where any person—(a) by his knowledge acquires a notifiable interest in shares in a listed company's relevant share capital, or ceases to be interested in such shares; or (b) becomes aware that he has acquired a notifiable interest in the relevant shares of a listed company or that he has ceased to be interested in such shares in which he was previously interested, such person is under an obligation to notify the listed company of the interest which he has, or had in its shares.
sections.relevance_reason: Record keeping for financial data

sections.heading: Furnishing of information to the Authority
sections.title: Information sharing with regulatory authorities
sections.summary: Regulated entities must provide specified information to the Authority in written form within specified timeframes, including sworn statements. The Authority may verify information and share it with other regulatory agencies under bilateral or multilateral agreements for enforcement and compliance purposes. Information obtained must be used for regulatory purposes and may be shared with other agencies as per agreements.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires regulated entities to share financial information with authorities, impacting data transfer and cross-border compliance with regulatory requirements.

requirements.text: Regulated entities must provide specified information to the Authority in written form within specified timeframes, including sworn statements
Authority may verify information and share it with other regulatory agencies under bilateral or multilateral agreements
Information obtained must be used for regulatory purposes and may be shared with other agencies as per agreements

sections.source_quote: Every person notified by the Authority pursuant to section 13 of the Act shall provide any specified information in the form and content as required by the Authority, with regards to the information on orders, purchases, sales or trading and settlement of securities including documentation relating to such transactions and disclosure of beneficial ownership of securities and such information may be shared with other regulatory agencies for the sole purpose of ensuring compliance, enforcement and any other matters pursuant to a bilateral or multilateral memorandum of understanding.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulatory information sharing with authorities

sections.heading: Preservation of financial and other records
sections.title: Preservation of financial records for seven years
sections.summary: Securities issuers and licensed persons must preserve all financial and transaction records in electronic or manual form for seven years, including daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, and annual transactions, meeting minutes, and security registers. This requirement applies to all transactions conducted by licensees or issuers.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates seven-year record retention for financial data, impacting data storage and cross-border data transfer compliance with retention periods.

requirements.text: Securities issuers and licensed persons must preserve all financial and transaction records in electronic or manual form for seven years

sections.source_quote: Every issuer of securities to the public or a section thereof approved by the Authority and every person licensed by the Authority, shall preserve all financial and other records whether such records are maintained in an electronic or manual form, relating to transactions conducted by the licensee or to the offer of securities by an issuer, including daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly and annual transactions and other relevant records including minutes of all meetings on account of such transactions and registers of securities, for a period of seven years.
sections.relevance_reason: Record keeping for financial data

sections.heading: Destruction of financial and other records
sections.title: Prohibition on destruction of financial records
sections.summary: No person may interfere, deface, or destroy financial records during the seven-year retention period, including records related to transactions by licensees or issuers, auditors, or professionals involved in transactions. This prohibition applies to all entities subject to record preservation requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Prohibits destruction of financial records for seven years, impacting data storage and cross-border data transfer compliance with record retention.

requirements.text: No person may interfere, deface, or destroy financial records during the seven-year retention period

sections.source_quote: No person shall at any time within the prescribed period interfere, deface or destroy the records referred to in regulation 77, in any manner that will lead to the alteration of any facts or content therein including the date, amount and names of all persons party to the transactions whether such person is a licensee of the Authority; an issuer of securities to the public or a section thereof, an auditor of such licensee or issuer or any professional who is or will be involved directly or indirectly in the transactions.
sections.relevance_reason: Record keeping for financial data

sections.heading: Prevention of money laundering and other illicit activities
sections.title: Client due diligence for money laundering prevention
sections.summary: Licensed persons must obtain client information through questionnaires covering identity, business activities, fund origins, and declarations of non-illicit sources. sections.heading: (1) FOR CORPORATE DEBT INSTRUMENTS (SECONDARY MARKET)
sections.title: Transaction fee structures for secondary market instruments
sections.summary: This section outlines the transaction fee structures for corporate debt and government debt instruments traded in the secondary market. It specifies the brokerage commission percentages and other fees payable by investors, including the CMA's percentage contribution to the investor compensation fund. These fees apply to market intermediaries such as stockbrokers and authorized securities dealers.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details fee structures for secondary market transactions, which may impact data transfer costs and financial reporting requirements for cross-border data flows involving financial instruments.

requirements.text: Market intermediaries trading in secondary market must charge 0.024% brokerage commission on corporate debt instruments
Market intermediaries must charge 0.0035% CMA fee on corporate debt instruments
Market intermediaries must charge 0.0015% CDSC fee on corporate debt instruments
Market intermediaries must charge 0.002% ICF fee on corporate debt instruments
Market intermediaries must charge 0.004% total transaction fees on corporate debt instruments
Market intermediaries must charge 0.0055% brokerage commission on government debt instruments
Market intermediaries must charge 0.015% CMA fee on government debt instruments
Market intermediaries must charge 0.004% ICF fee on government debt instruments
Market intermediaries must charge 0.035% total transaction fees on government debt instruments
Market intermediaries must contribute 0.004% of transaction value to the investor compensation fund

sections.heading: Private Transfer Fees
sections.title: Private transfer fee structures for securities transactions
sections.summary: This section specifies private transfer fee structures for certificated and immobilized securities transactions. It details the fee percentages for different types of transactions, including the application fees and the regulatory requirements for transfers involving changes in beneficial interest in share capital. These fees apply to market intermediaries and are subject to specific conditions and maximum limits.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines fee structures for securities transfers, which may impact data transfer costs and financial reporting requirements for cross-border data flows involving financial instruments.

requirements.text: Certificated securities transactions must charge 55% fee to brokers and 45% to investment banks
Immobilized securities transactions must charge 55% fee to brokers and 45% to investment banks
Regulation 57(a) fees levied at 2.1% of transaction value (maximum 1.7%) for transactions below Kshs. 100,000
Regulation 57(b) or (e) fees of Kshs. 1,500 per application (minimum fee of Kshs. 1,500 for transactions above Kshs. 10,000)
Regulation 57(c) requires 0.1% fee on nominal value of shares for transfers without change in beneficial interest
Regulation 57(c) or (d) requires 0.5% fee on market value of shares for transfers with change in beneficial interest
